title: Reverse a Linked List
question: How do you reverse a singly linked list in-place?
expected_answer: Iterate through the list, changing the next pointer of each node to point to the previous node.
hints: Consider the core principles of Reverse a Linked List

title: Detect Cycle
question: How to detect a cycle in a linked list?
expected_answer: Use two pointers, slow and fast.
hints: Consider the core principles of Detect Cycle

title: Binary Search
question: Implement binary search on a sorted array.
expected_answer: Find middle, if target < mid search left, else search right.
hints: Consider the core principles of Binary Search

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
question: Merge k sorted linked lists into one sorted list.
expected_answer: Use a min-heap to keep track of the smallest current element.
hints: Consider the core principles of Merge K Sorted Lists

title: LRU Cache
question: Design an LRU Cache with O(1) get and put.
expected_answer: Use a dict for fast lookup and a doubly linked list for fast removal.
hints: Consider the core principles of LRU Cache

title: Valid Parentheses
question: Check if a string containing parentheses '()[]{{}}' is valid.
expected_answer: Use a stack to push opening brackets and pop/check closing brackets.
hints: Consider the core principles of Valid Parentheses

title: Two Sum
question: Find two numbers in an array that add up to a target sum.
expected_answer: Store seen numbers in a hash map mapping value to index.
hints: Consider the core principles of Two Sum

title: Longest Substring Without Repeats
question: Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
expected_answer: Use a sliding window and a set/dict to track seen characters.
hints: Consider the core principles of Longest Substring Without Repeats

title: Polymorphism
question: Explain Polymorphism in object-oriented programming with an example.
expected_answer: The ability of different objects to respond to the same method call in their own specific way.
hints: Consider the core principles of Polymorphism

title: Inheritance vs Composition
question: What is the difference between inheritance and composition? When would you use each?
expected_answer: Inheritance represents an IS-A relationship, whereas Composition represents a HAS-A relationship. Favor composition for flexibility.
hints: Consider the core principles of Inheritance vs Composition

title: Encapsulation
question: What is encapsulation and how does it promote maintainability?
expected_answer: Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit/class while restricting direct access to internal state.
hints: Consider the core principles of Encapsulation

title: Abstract Class vs Interface
question: What are the key differences between an Abstract Class and an Interface?
expected_answer: Abstract classes can have instance state and default method bodies; interfaces define contracts.
hints: Consider the core principles of Abstract Class vs Interface

title: SOLID Principles
question: Explain the SOLID principles of object-oriented software design.
expected_answer: Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion.
hints: Consider the core principles of SOLID Principles

title: Singleton Pattern
question: Explain the Singleton design pattern, its implementation, and potential drawbacks.
expected_answer: Ensures a class has only one instance. Drawbacks include hidden global dependencies and tight coupling.
hints: Consider the core principles of Singleton Pattern

title: Factory Pattern
question: Explain the Factory Method Pattern and how it promotes loose coupling.
expected_answer: Delegates object instantiation to subclasses without specifying exact concrete classes.
hints: Consider the core principles of Factory Pattern

title: Method Overloading vs Overriding
question: Explain the difference between method overloading and method overriding.
expected_answer: Overloading is compile-time polymorphism (same method, different parameters). Overriding is runtime polymorphism (redefining a superclass method).
hints: Consider the core principles of Method Overloading vs Overriding

title: ACID Properties
question: What are the ACID properties in database transactions?
expected_answer: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state transitions), Isolation (concurrent transaction safety), Durability (persisted changes).
hints: Consider the core principles of ACID Properties

title: Database Indexing
question: How do B-Tree database indexes improve query performance?
expected_answer: Indexes create balanced search trees on indexed columns, reducing query time complexity from O(N) sequential scan to O(log N).
hints: Consider the core principles of Database Indexing

title: Database Normalization
question: What is database normalization and why is 3NF commonly targeted?
expected_answer: Organizing columns and tables to minimize data redundancy and insertion/deletion anomalies.
hints: Consider the core principles of Database Normalization

title: SQL vs NoSQL
question: Compare relational (SQL) databases with document-based (NoSQL) databases.
expected_answer: SQL provides structured schema and ACID transactions; NoSQL provides flexible schema and horizontal scaling.
hints: Consider the core principles of SQL vs NoSQL

title: SQL Joins
question: Explain the differences between INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL OUTER JOINs.
expected_answer: INNER returns matching rows in both tables; LEFT returns all rows from left table plus matched right rows; FULL returns all rows when there is a match in either.
sample_code: SELECT * FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id;
hints: Consider the core principles of SQL Joins

title: Database Transactions
question: What is a database transaction and how do savepoints work?
expected_answer: A logical unit of work executed atomically. Savepoints allow rolling back to intermediate states.
hints: Consider the core principles of Database Transactions

title: Deadlocks in Databases
question: What causes database deadlocks and how can database systems prevent or detect them?
expected_answer: Occurs when two transactions hold locks wanted by the other. Systems detect via wait-for graphs or prevent via lock ordering.
hints: Consider the core principles of Deadlocks in Databases

title: CAP Theorem
question: Explain the CAP theorem in distributed database design.
expected_answer: A distributed data store can simultaneously provide at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance.
hints: Consider the core principles of CAP Theorem

title: Process vs Thread
question: What is the key difference between a process and a thread?
expected_answer: A process has its own isolated memory space. Threads share memory space within the parent process.
hints: Consider the core principles of Process vs Thread

title: Virtual Memory & Paging
question: Explain how virtual memory and page tables work in modern operating systems.
expected_answer: Translates virtual addresses to physical RAM addresses via page tables and translation lookaside buffers (TLB).
hints: Consider the core principles of Virtual Memory & Paging

title: Mutex vs Semaphore
question: Compare Mutex and Semaphore synchronization primitives.
expected_answer: A Mutex is a locking mechanism (owned by one thread). A Semaphore is a signaling mechanism using a counter.
hints: Consider the core principles of Mutex vs Semaphore

title: OSI Model Layers
question: Describe the 7 layers of the OSI networking model.
expected_answer: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.
hints: Consider the core principles of OSI Model Layers

title: TCP vs UDP
question: Compare TCP and UDP transport layer protocols.
expected_answer: TCP is connection-oriented, reliable, ordered. UDP is connectionless, unreliable, low-latency.
hints: Consider the core principles of TCP vs UDP

title: DNS Resolution
question: Explain step-by-step how DNS resolution converts a domain name to an IP address.
expected_answer: Browser cache -> OS cache -> Recursive Resolver -> Root Server -> TLD Server -> Authoritative Nameserver.
hints: Consider the core principles of DNS Resolution

title: TCP 3-Way Handshake
question: Explain the TCP three-way handshake process.
expected_answer: SYN (Client to Server) -> SYN-ACK (Server to Client) -> ACK (Client to Server).
hints: Consider the core principles of TCP 3-Way Handshake

title: System Calls & Context Switching
question: What occurs during a CPU context switch initiated by a system call?
expected_answer: CPU switches from user mode to kernel mode, saves register state, loads the target process/thread register state.
hints: Consider the core principles of System Calls & Context Switching

title: URL Shortener
question: How would you design a scalable URL shortener service like Bitly?
expected_answer: Use base62 encoding of auto-incrementing IDs or MD5 hashes, a key-value store (Redis/Cassandra) for fast redirects, and a load balancer.
hints: Consider the core principles of URL Shortener

title: Load Balancing
question: Explain load balancing algorithms (Round Robin, Least Connections, Consistent Hashing).
expected_answer: Round Robin rotates sequentially; Least Connections routes to least busy node; Consistent Hashing minimizes key remapping on cluster changes.
hints: Consider the core principles of Load Balancing

title: Caching Strategies
question: Compare Cache-Aside, Write-Through, Write-Behind, and Refresh-Ahead caching patterns.
expected_answer: Cache-Aside reads cache first then DB; Write-Through updates cache and DB synchronously; Write-Behind updates DB asynchronously.
hints: Consider the core principles of Caching Strategies

title: Database Sharding
question: What is database sharding and how do you handle cross-shard queries?
expected_answer: Horizontal partitioning of database rows across multiple machines by shard key.
hints: Consider the core principles of Database Sharding

title: Message Queues & Pub/Sub
question: When and why would you use an event-driven architecture with Kafka or RabbitMQ?
expected_answer: Decouples producers and consumers, buffers high throughput traffic spikes, enables async event processing.
hints: Consider the core principles of Message Queues & Pub/Sub

title: API Rate Limiting
question: Design an API rate limiter using the Token Bucket or Sliding Window algorithm.
expected_answer: Token bucket adds tokens at fixed rate; requests consume tokens. Sliding window log counts timestamps in Redis.
hints: Consider the core principles of API Rate Limiting

title: Microservices Architecture
question: Discuss the trade-offs of Microservices vs Monolithic architecture.
expected_answer: Microservices allow independent deployment and scaling but introduce distributed complexity, network latency, and eventual consistency.
hints: Consider the core principles of Microservices Architecture

title: Distributed Tracing
question: How do you trace user requests across dozens of microservices?
expected_answer: Inject trace and span IDs in HTTP headers (OpenTelemetry/Jaeger) to track call trees across services.
hints: Consider the core principles of Distributed Tracing

title: Technical Conflict
question: Describe a technical disagreement you had with a teammate and how you resolved it.
expected_answer: STAR method: Situation, Task, Action (listened, gathered data, benchmarked), Result (reached consensus).
hints: Consider the core principles of Technical Conflict

title: Handling Failure
question: Tell me about a time a project or deployment failed. What did you learn?
expected_answer: STAR method: Owned responsibility, ran post-mortem, introduced automated tests/monitoring to prevent recurrence.
hints: Consider the core principles of Handling Failure

title: Technical Leadership
question: Describe a situation where you took initiative to improve a system or engineering process.
expected_answer: Identified bottleneck/tech debt, proposed solution to team, built prototype, led adoption.
hints: Consider the core principles of Technical Leadership

title: Tight Deadlines & Scope
question: How do you handle a situation where a feature deadline cannot be met with full scope?
expected_answer: Communicated early with stakeholders, identified MVP/core path, deferred non-critical subfeatures.
hints: Consider the core principles of Tight Deadlines & Scope

title: Receiving Critical Feedback
question: Describe a time you received constructive feedback on your code or performance.
expected_answer: Listened without defensiveness, applied suggestions, followed up to verify improvement.
hints: Consider the core principles of Receiving Critical Feedback

title: Disagree and Commit
question: Tell me about a time you disagreed with an architectural decision made by leadership.
expected_answer: Presented evidence-based alternative, accepted final decision gracefully, committed 100% to execution.
hints: Consider the core principles of Disagree and Commit

title: Mentoring Teammates
question: How have you helped onboard or mentor junior engineers on your team?
expected_answer: Pair programming, thorough code reviews, creating documentation and onboarding guides.
hints: Consider the core principles of Mentoring Teammates

title: Learning New Tech
question: Tell me about a time you had to learn an unfamiliar technology under time pressure.
expected_answer: Broke down requirements, built small proof-of-concept, leveraged documentation, delivered on time.
hints: Consider the core principles of Learning New Tech

title: REST vs GraphQL
question: Compare RESTful API design with GraphQL for frontend-backend communication.
expected_answer: REST uses fixed endpoints per resource; GraphQL uses single endpoint allowing clients to request exact fields.
hints: Consider the core principles of REST vs GraphQL

title: Docker Containerization
question: How does Docker container isolation differ from full virtual machines?
expected_answer: Containers share the host OS kernel and isolate user space using cgroups/namespaces; VMs run full guest OS on hypervisor.
hints: Consider the core principles of Docker Containerization

title: CI/CD Pipeline
question: What are the core stages of a production CI/CD pipeline?
expected_answer: Lint -> Unit Tests -> Build Artifact -> Integration Tests -> Staging Deploy -> Production Deploy.
hints: Consider the core principles of CI/CD Pipeline

title: Web Security (OWASP Top 10)
question: Explain XSS, CSRF, and SQL Injection vulnerabilities and their preventions.
expected_answer: XSS: sanitize input/escape output; CSRF: use anti-CSRF tokens/SameSite cookies; SQLi: use parameterized queries.
hints: Consider the core principles of Web Security (OWASP Top 10)

title: OAuth2 & JWT
question: How does OAuth2 authentication flow work with JWT access tokens?
expected_answer: Client authenticates with Auth Provider, receives signed JWT containing user claims/exp, passes Bearer token to API.
hints: Consider the core principles of OAuth2 & JWT

title: React Rendering & Virtual DOM
question: Explain how React's Virtual DOM and reconciliation algorithm optimize rendering.
expected_answer: React keeps virtual UI tree, computes diff (reconciliation) on state change, applies minimal DOM mutations.
hints: Consider the core principles of React Rendering & Virtual DOM

title: Async/Await & Event Loop
question: Explain the event loop and non-blocking I/O model in Node.js or Python asyncio.
expected_answer: Single thread runs event loop checking task queue/callbacks; I/O operations delegated to OS kernel/worker threads.
hints: Consider the core principles of Async/Await & Event Loop

title: Kubernetes Pods & Services
question: What is a Kubernetes Pod and how does a Service route traffic to Pods?
expected_answer: Pod is smallest deployable unit (1+ containers). Service uses label selectors to load balance traffic across Pod IPs.
hints: Consider the core principles of Kubernetes Pods & Services
